SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 1 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 || Plaintiff alleges as follows:
2 JURISDICTIONAL STATEMENT
; 1. This is an action for damages as to: (1) Age Discrimination; (2) Wrongful
Termination in Violation of Public Policy; (3) Retaliation - FEHA; (4) Retaliation - ADA; (5)
6 || Harassment; (6) Breach of Contract; (7) Breach of Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair
7 || Dealing; (8) Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress and (9) Failure to Investigate. This
8 action arises out of events involving Plaintiff PATRICIA WESTERFIELD and defendants
. TRUSTEES OF THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO, an unknown business entity,
Tl UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO, a corporation doing business in California, B.J.
12. || JOHNSON, an individual, DENA DAVIS, an individual, NICOLE BOHN, an individual and
13 || DOES 1 -50. The unlawful conduct which gives rise to this lawsuit occurred within the City of
14 San Francisco, California, in the County of San Francisco, and therefore arose within this
judicial district.
17 THE PARTIES
18 2. Plaintiff PATRICIA WESTERFIELD (hereafter “Plaintiff” or
, “WESTERFIELD”) is a 52 year old female who was formerly employed by defendants
41 TRUSTEES OF THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO and UNIVERSITY OF SAN
49 || FRANCISCO as an Instructional Designer and Coordinator of Academic Accommodations.
23 3. Plaintiff WESTERFIELD began working with defendants on or about May 1995
a4 through May 2002. Plaintiff exhausted her administrative remedies by timely filing a complaint
. with the Department of Fair Employment and Housing against each defendant and has received a
17 right to sue letter. WESTERFIELD also exhausted her administrative remedies by timely filing
28 ||a complaint with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (hereafter “EEOC”).
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 2 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 4. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant
2 || UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO (hereafter “USF”) is a private Jesuit university, which is
3
registered as a California corporation.
4
5 5. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant TRUSTEES
6 ||OF THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO (hereafter “TRUSTEES”) are an unknown
? business entity, which oversees university affairs.\
8
9 6. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant B. J.
10 || JOHNSON (hereafter “JOHNSON”) is Dean of Academic Services for USF.
11
7. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant DENA
12
3 DAVIS (hereafter “DAVIS”) is Director of Academic Support Services for USF.
14 8. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant NICOLE
15
BOHN (hereafter “BOHN”) is Coordinator/Associate Director of Disability Services for USF.
16
17 9. Plaintiff is ignorant of the true names and capacities of the defendants sued herein
18 ||as DOES 1 through 50, inclusive, and therefore sues them by such fictitious names. Plaintiff is
19 informed and believes and thereon alleges that said defendants are in some manner legally
20
responsible for the activities and damages alleged herein. Plaintiff will amend this Complaint to
21
yy) allege their true names and capacities when ascertained.
23 10. _ Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that at all times herein
24
mentioned each of the defendants was acting as the partner, agent, servant, and employee of each
25
06 of the remaining defendants, and in doing the things alleged herein was acting within the course
77 |\and scope of such agency and with the knowledge of the remaining defendants.
28 GENERAL ALLEGATIONS
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 3 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 11. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that the principal office and
2 headquarters for all defendants is in San Francisco, California, San Francisco County.
3
4 12. _ Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that she began her
5 ||employment with USF in May 1995. Over the course of WESTERFIELD’s seven years of
6 || employment with defendants, she experienced a steady succession of promotions.
u WESTERFIELD began as a reader for students with visual disabilities. However, little more
8
9 than a year later, Plaintiff was perceived to be doing so well that she was promoted to designing
10 and coordinating educational accommodations for those same disabled students as well as
11 || tutoring and teaching disabled students to use adaptive equipment. In June 1999, Plaintiff was
12 promoted yet again to and Instructional Designer and Coordinator of Academic
13 .
Accommodations.
14
15 13. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendants began to
16 || humiliate and verbally abuse WESTERFIELD after she was promoted to Instructional Designer
17 || and Coordinator of Academic Accommodations and in June 1999 outside the scope of her
18 employment. Defendant BOHN, a disabled woman in her mid-twenties, yelled at plaintiff and
19
ordered her to perform menial tasks, which were not part of her job description. BOHN also
20
41 refused to communicate with WESTERFIELD by ignoring her and refusing to respond to
97 || communications attempted by Plaintiff.
23 14. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendants made
24 repeated derogatory age related comments to WESTERFIELD regarding menopause and not
25
being able to think as well as younger people. Defendants made additional derogatory age
26
7 related comments as to old timers, battleaxes and older people not thinking as fast.
28
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 4 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 15. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendant BOHN
2 |\ forced WESTERFIELD to perform her job responsibilities. BOHN also had the audacity to take
; credit for the work that she forced WESTERFIELD to complete. BOHN also yelled at Plaintiff
and humiliated her in front of others. Plaintiff was intentionally excluded from meetings and a
6 || work related conference and excluded from access to information needed in order to fulfill her
7 || job responsibilities in contrast to her co-workers. Defendants also failed to acknowledge
8 WESTERFIELD’s birthday, also, in contrast to her co-workers.
. 16. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that WESTERFIELD
1 attempted to work around the egregious conduct of BOHN. However, BOHN’s outrageous
12 || behavior became unbearable. Plaintiff complained to defendant DAVIS in April 2001.
13 || However, DAVIS completely failed to take steps in order to correct BOHN’s wrongful conduct
M4 against WESTERFIELD.
: 17. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that subsequent to
17 WESTERFIELD’s complaints, BOHN failed to either have or complete meetings which
18 || addressed Plaintiffs performance evaluation, which included several unfounded negative
19 |) comments by BOHN. Despite BOHN’s attempt to sabotage WESTERFIELD at USF, Plaintiff
20 received a merit pay increase, but it served to only cause BOHN’s hostility to escalate. BOHN’s
i. aggression towards WESTERFIELD was witnessed by others.
23 18. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that WESTERFIELD
24 || discovered in February 2000 that defendant USF was not in compliance with the directives of the
25 Americans with Disabilities Act (hereafter “ADA”) as to many different types of
. accommodations. The sub-standard accommodations concerned copiers, computers and tables.
28
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 5 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 || Disabled students at USF were being deprived access to equipment that they needed to fulfill

2 || their educational objectives despite the fact that USF was received government funding.

; 19. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that she received a

satisfactory evaluation for her work performance at USF in May 2000. WESTERFIELD also

6 || became aware that defendants did not have a required “504 transition plan” pursuant to ADA

7 || directives. Plaintiff continued to be actively involved in attempting to bring defendants into

8 compliance with the ADA. WESTERFIELD notified BOHN and others of the ADA compliance
. violations, but defendants’ hostility towards her only increased. WESTERFIELD’s informing
7 defendants of their ADA non-compliance culminated with negative comments on her May 2001
12 || performance evaluation. It was defendants’ policy to allow its employees adequate opportunities
13 || to review and make comments. However, Plaintiff was barely given one hour to review and

14 sign. BOHN also cancelled and was late to multiple meetings that were scheduled by Plaintiff
: regarding her evaluation.

7 20. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that she assumed that

18 || defendants wanted to comply with the law and, thus, continued to attempt to work with them to
19 bring USF into ADA compliance. However, defendants only branded her as a trouble maker and
20 terminated her. Defendant JOHNSON participated in a hoax to remove WESTERFIELD from
. her position by pretending that her job was being eliminated because student numbers, needs and
33 || accommodations had changed.

24 21. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that WESTERFIELD was
25 given a letter of termination in February 2002. The letter stated that her position was being

i. terminated because her department was being reorganized in order to better meet increased

28

SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 6 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 {| student demand. When Plaintiff’s position was terminated, the program and funding remained
2 || the same. Two new positions were created from Plaintiff's position.
; 22. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that WESTERFIELD was
given letters of recommendation by defendant JOHNSON and DAVIS and told that she could
6 |\apply for another position within the university. JOHNSON told others that Plaintiff would be
7 || invited to apply for one of the positions that derived from her previous position. However, that
8 never happened. Defendants had no intention of honoring their statements. Plaintiff applied for
. an Assistant Coordinator of Disability Services position in April 2002. That position was
Wl essentially the same position that she held previous. However, WESTERFIELD was denied an
12 || interview.
13 23. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendants then offered
M4 the position to two women under the age of 40. Plaintiff notified defendants in writing of her
: belief that she had been discriminated against as to the denial of an interview, but defendants did
17 nothing. Defendants then went outside of their normal practices to hire an out-of-state person
18 || from Illinois for a position which had derived from WESTERFIELD’s former job. That
19 || woman’s qualifications were inferior to those of Plaintiff.
20 24. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that she entered into a
. contract regarding her employment with USF on or about June 1, 1999. Defendants told plaintifi
93 || that her employment with them would continue as long as they had disabled students.
24 || WESTERFIELD was given a 12-month probationary period, which, if completed, deemed that
25 she would not be an at will employee. Defendants further indicated that WESTERFIELD would
i. have employment forever - as long as USF had disabled students.
28 Hl
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 7 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
: , —_ -
1 FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION - AGE DISCRIMINATION
2 AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
° Cal. Govt. Code § 12940
; 25. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations of paragraphs 1 through
6 || 24 above.
7 26. Defendants, through their agents and employees engaged in a pattern and practice
8 of unlawful age discrimination in violation of the Fair Employment and Housing Act (hereinafter
. referred to as "FEHA") in connection with plaintiffs work assignments, training, and the terms
W and conditions of plaintiffs' employment.
12 27.  Atall relevant times, defendants had actual and constructive knowledge of the
13 || discriminatory conduct described and alleged herein, and condoned, ratified and participated in
4 the discrimination. As a result of the hostile and offensive work environment perpetrated and
. maintained by defendants, and defendants’ failure to protect plaintiff from further discrimination,
17 Plaintiff suffered severe emotional distress.
18 28. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that in addition to the
19 practices enumerated above, defendants, and each of them, engaged in other discriminatory
20 practices against Plaintiff, which are not yet fully known. At such time as said discriminatory
. practices become known to Plaintiff, Plaintiff will seek leave of court to amend this complaint in
73 || those regards.
24 29. As a direct and proximate result of the willful, knowing, and intentional
25 |) discrimination against Plaintiff, and the failure to act by defendants, Plaintiff has suffered mental
i. distress, anguish, and indignation. Plaintiff is thereby entitled to general and compensatory
28 damages in an amount to be proven at trial.
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 8 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 30. | Defendants’ acts alleged herein are malicious, oppressive, despicable, and in

2 || conscious disregard of plaintiff’s rights. As such, punitive damages are warranted against

; defendants in order to punish and make an example of their actions.

SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION - WRONGFUL TERMINATION

6 IN VIOLATION OF PUBLIC POLICY - AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND

7 TRUSTEES

8 31. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1

9 || through 30 herein.
10 32.  Plaintiff’s wrongful termination from her employment with USF was based upon
. defendants’ violation of public policy, including but not limited to the following: the
B fundamental public policy against age discrimination, retaliation and the fundamental public
14 || policy of equal opportunity employment.
15 33. Asa proximate result of defendants’ wrongful acts, Plaintiff has suffered and
16 continues to suffer substantial losses incurred in seeking substitute employment and in earnings,
. bonuses, deferred compensation, stock options, and other employment benefits; and has suffered
19 and continues to suffer emotional distress in an amount according to proof at the time of trial.
20 34. Defendants, and each of them, did the acts alleged herein maliciously,
21 fraudulently and oppressively, with the wrongful intent to injure Plaintiff, from an improper and
22 evil motive amounting to malice, and in conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights. The acts
. complained of were known to, authorized and ratified by defendants. Plaintiff is therefore
5 || entitled to recover punitive damages from defendants, and each of them, in an amount according
26 || to proof at the time of trial.

27

28

SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 9 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION — RETALIATION IN VIOLATION
2 OF CALIFORNIA GOVERNMENT CODE §12940 -
° AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
35. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1
6 || through 34 herein.
? 36. Under California Government Code §12940 defendants are prohibited from
retaliation against Plaintiff for opposing any practices forbidden or made unlawful under
10 California Government Code §12940.
11 37. Plaintiff complained to defendants on numerous occasions alleged herein that she
12 || was being discriminated against and harassed on the basis of age.
13 38. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendants had
. numerous opportunities to remedy the hostility in their work place, but chose to increase the
16 || discrimination and harassment of WESTERFIELD instead. Plaintiff was engaged in protected
17 || activity for which defendants retaliated.
18 39.  Atall relevant times, defendants had actual and constructive knowledge of the
. retaliatory conduct described and alleged herein, and condoned, ratified and participated in the
. retaliation. As a result of the retaliatory behavior perpetrated and executed by defendants, and
77, || defendants’ failure to protect Plaintiff from retaliatory behavior, Plaintiff has suffered severe
23 || emotional distress, humiliation and embarrassment. As a further proximate result of such
a4 conduct, Plaintiff has suffered loss of income, loss of advance and promotions, loss of career
. opportunity and loss of tangible job benefits, all in amounts to be proven at trial.
27
28

1 40. Defendants’ acts alleged herein are malicious, oppressive, fraudulent, despicable,
2 |) and in conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights and public policy. As such, punitive damages are
; warranted against defendants in order to punish and make an example out of each of them.
FOURTH CAUSE OF ACTION — RETALIATION IN VIOLATION OF THE ADA
6 AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
7 41. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1
8 through 40 herein.
9
10 42. Under the ADA, defendants are prohibited from retaliating against Plaintiff for

12 43. Plaintiff repeatedly informed defendants that they were not in compliance with
13 ADA guidelines as to their disabled students.
. 44. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that defendants had
16 || Humerous opportunities to remedy their failure to comply with the ADA, but chose to ignore
17 || WESTERFIELD’s efforts. Additionally, defendants escalated their retaliation against Plaintiff
18 ll for informing them of their non-ADA compliance, which culminated in their wrongfully
? terminating Plaintiff

1 45.  Atall relevant times, defendants had actual and constructive knowledge of the
79 |j|retaliatory conduct described and alleged herein, and condoned, ratified and participated in the
23 || retaliation. As a result of the retaliatory behavior perpetrated and executed by defendants, and
24 defendants’ failure to protect Plaintiff from retaliatory behavior, Plaintiff has suffered severe
. emotional distress, humiliation and embarrassment. As a further proximate result of such

1 46. Defendants’ acts alleged herein are malicious, oppressive, fraudulent, despicable,
2 || and in conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights and public policy. As such, punitive damages are
° warranted against defendants in order to punish and make an example out of each of them.
FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION —- HARASSMENT
6 IN VIOLATION OF CALIFORNIA GOVERNMENT CODE §12940
7 AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF, TRUSTEES AND BOHN
8 47. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations of paragraphs | through
° 46 herein.
10
11 48. By engaging in the conduct previously alleged herein, defendants, and each of
12 || them, violated California Government Code § 12940 by creating, maintaining, encouraging,
13 || failing to investigate properly and by failing to remedy a hostile work environment prohibited by
4 the Fair Employment and Housing Act, despite Plaintiffs complaints to management level
° employees that she was being harassed and excluded because of Plaintiff's age. Plaintiff was
17 also given an increased work load
18 49, Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that the working conditions
19 |) at USF were both subjectively and objectively pervasive as to constitute a prima facie case of
20 harassment under state laws. Plaintiff's harassment was made aware to management level
. employees at USF. Defendants’ management failed to discipline, admonish, or timely
73 || investigate plaintiff's claims. Instead of prompt investigation or resolution of the hostile
24 || environment, defendants, through their management level employees and agents, chose to
25 |) escalate harassment of WESTERFIELD and then terminate her.
. 50. At all relevant times, defendants had actual and constructive knowledge of the
28 hostile work environment described and alleged herein, and condoned, ratified and failed to
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 12 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 || remedy in the hostile work environment. As a result of the hostile work environment perpetrated
2 |! and maintained by defendants, and defendants’ failure to protect Plaintiff from further
3 discrimination, Plaintiff has suffered severe emotional distress, humiliation, indignity and loss of
income in an amount provable at the time of trial.
6 51. Plaintiff is informed and believes and thereon alleges that in addition to the
7 || practices enumerated above, defendants, and each of them, have engaged in other discriminatory
8 practices against Plaintiff, which are not yet fully known. At such time as said discriminatory
. practices become known to Plaintiff, Plaintiff will seek leave of court to amend this complaint in
7 those regards.
12 52. Asa direct and proximate result of the willful, knowing and intentional
13 || harassment of Plaintiff, and.defendants’ failure to act in an effort to remedy said environment,
4 plaintiff has suffered mental distress, anguish and indignation. Plaintiff is thereby entitled to
’ general and compensatory damages in an amount to be proven at trial.
17 53. Defendants’ acts alleged herein are malicious, oppressive, despicable, and in
18 || conscious disregard of Plaintiff’s rights. As such, punitive damages are warranted against
19 || defendants in order to punish and make an example of each of them.
20 SIXTH CAUSE OF ACTION —- BREACH OF CONTRACT
. AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
23 54. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1
74 || through 53 herein.
25 55. Plaintiff was employed by defendant USF pursuant to a contract.
26 56.  Atall relevant times alleged herein, Plaintiff performed her obligations under her
contract with defendants. Defendants by and through their agents and employees, breached both
its oral and contractual commitments to Plaintiff by failing to provide Plaintiff with employment
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 13 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 || pursuant to its own representations and by creating, maintaining and ratifying hostile working
2 || conditions which were in violation of the statutes, ordinances, regulations, or public policy of the
; State of California.
57. Plaintiff justifiably relied upon the oral and written representations of defendants.
6 58. Asadirect and proximate result of defendants’ breach, Plaintiff has suffered loss
7 || of wages and benefits, loss of future wages and benefits and emotional in an amount to be prove
8 at the time of trial.
. SEVENTH CAUSE OF ACTION — BREACH OF IMPLIED
7 COVENANT OF GOOD FAITH AND FAIR DEALING
12 AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
13 59. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1
14 || through 58 herein.
IS 60. Implied in every contract is a Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing. Ail
. parties to a contract owe each other a duty to refrain from acts, which would prevent or unduly
1g || hinder the other party’s performance of the contract.
19 61. Defendants, by and through their agents and employees, breached this covenant
20 by subjecting Plaintiff to work conditions that were in violation of the contract, statutes,
i. ordinances, regulations, and public policy of the State of California and by wrongfully
53 terminating Plaintiff.
24 62. Absent defendants’ decision, through their agents and employees, to make
25 || Plaintiff work in conditions that Defendant created, maintained and ratified, which were in
26 violation of the statutes, ordinances, regulations, and public policy of the State of California, and
absent defendants’ decision to wrongfully terminate Plaintiff, Plaintiff would have fully
performed her job duties.
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 14 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 63. Defendants’ actions thereby constitute a breach of the Implied Covenant of Good
2 || Faith and Fair Dealing and Plaintiff is entitled to general and compensatory damages in an
; amount provable at the time of trial.
EIGHTH CAUSE OF ACTION — INTENTIONAL INFLICTION
6 OF EMOTIONAL DISTRESS — AGAINST ALL DEFENDANTS
7 64. Plaintiff incorporates by reference the factual allegations set forth in paragraphs 1
8 through 63 herein.
. 65. Plaintiff'is informed and believes and thereon alleges that on or about April of
1 2000, and continuing to present, defendants, by and through its principals, agents and employees,
12 |j unlawfully harassed, retaliated, terminated WESTERFIELD in violation of public policy and
13 |) applicable law, with conscious disregard of the result or outcome of such act. The unlawful
14 harassment, retaliation, and termination of WESTERFIELD and defendants’ denial of Plaintiff's
. promotion, due to its improper motivations and surrounding circumstances constitute extreme
17 and outrageous conduct by the defendants, and each of them.
18 66. Through the outrageous conduct described above, defendants acted with the intent
19 No cause, and with reckless disregard for the probability of causing plaintiff to suffer severe
20 emotional distress.
21
9 67. At all relevant times, defendants had actual or constructive knowledge of extreme
23 || and outrageous conduct described herein, and condoned, ratified and participated in such
24 || extreme and outrageous acts.
25 68. As a direct and proximate result of defendants’ willful, knowing and intentional
. acts, and defendants’ failure to act, Plaintiff has suffered and will continue to suffer mental
28 distress and anguish. Plaintiff has suffered and will continue to suffer a loss of earnings, stock
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 15 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 |/ options, and other employment benefits and job opportunities. Plaintiff is thereby entitled to
2 general and compensatory damages in amount to be proven at trial.
; 69. The acts of defendants, as alleged herein, were done with fraud, oppression and
malice, with a conscious disregard for Plaintiff's rights; and with the intent, design and purpose
6 || of injuring Plaintiff, with an improper and evil motive amounting to malice, in conscious
7 || disregard of Plaintiff's rights. Plaintiff is therefore entitled to recover punitive damages against
8 defendants, and each of them.
. NINTH CAUSE OF ACTION — FAILURE TO INVESTIGATE
11 IN VIOLATION OF GOVERNMENT CODE SECTION 12940 —
12 AGAINST DEFENDANTS USF AND TRUSTEES
13 70. Plaintiff hereby adopts and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1 through 69,
. inclusive, of this complaint and makes them a part of this instant cause of action as though fully
16 || Se forth herein.
17 71. The California Fair Employment and Housing Act, (“FEHA”) as amended,
18 |! Government Code Section 12940 makes it an unlawful employment practice for an employer to
19 fail to take all reasonable steps necessary to prevent discrimination from occurring.
. 72. By defendants’ conduct, as aforesaid, and by failing to express strong disapproval
22 || of discriminatory practices, by failing to develop appropriate sanctions for discrimination, by
23 || failing to properly inform Plaintiff and other employees of their rights regarding discrimination
24 and instructing Plaintiff and other employees of the specific process to follow in order to report
. discrimination, by failing to develop appropriate methods to sensitize employees/managers to
7 practices and the gravity of consequences for doing so, and by failing to take immediate and
28 || appropriate corrective action to stop the discrimination from occurring.
SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 16 CASE NO. CGC-03-417577

 
1 73. As a proximate result of defendants’ unlawful employment practices, Plaintiff has
2 || suffered damages, including but not limited to damages for wage loss and loss of employment
3
benefits, as well as damages for humiliation, embarrassment, damages for severe mental and
4
5 emotional distress and discomfort, as well as forcing him to incur attorneys’ fees, costs and
6 || certain other incidental damages, all according to proof.
7 74. Defendants committed the acts alleged herein maliciously, fraudulently, and
8 oppressively, and with the wrongful intention of injuring Plaintiff, and acted with an improper
9
and evil motive amounting to malice. Alternatively, defendants’ despicable conduct was carried
10
i out in conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights. Defendants’ conduct was carried out by one of
12 || their managing agents who had advance knowledge of the unfitness of its decision-maker and
13 || employed them with a conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights and/or authorized and/or ratified
M4 their conduct. As a result of defendants’ conduct, Plaintiff is entitled to recover punitive
15
6 damages in an amount commensurate with defendants’ wealth.

18
